literal: Lord-soul-bond; from pati ("Lord"), paśu ("bound soul; literally 'animal'"), pāśa ("bond; tether")
invariant_definition: The triadic ontology of the Pāśupata Śaiva tradition (and, in inherited form, of the dualist Kashmirian Saiddhāntika tradition): three eternally distinct categories — *pati* (the Lord, identified with Śiva, the *nimitta-kāraṇa* or efficient cause of the manifest world), *paśu* (the bound soul, possessed of its own *cetanā* and *kartṛtva* and *bhoktṛtva*, eternally distinct from *pati* and from other *paśus*), *pāśa* (the bond, comprising *karma*, *māyā*, and embodiment, that binds the *paśu* to the cycle of births). The triad is the position the *Brahma-Sūtra* refutes at 2.2.37 as the *pāśupata-pakṣa*. The Vedāntic refutation argues that Śiva-as-*nimitta-kāraṇa*-only (without being *upādāna-kāraṇa*) fails to give a coherent account of the Lord's relation to the world: either world arises without material cause, or some other material cause is needed (in which case the Lord's lordship is qualified), or the Lord himself is *upādāna* (in which case the Pāśupata position collapses into a Vedāntic position). The Saiddhāntika (Sadyojyotis, Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha) inherit the *pati-paśu-pāśa* triad and develop it with the *kalā-tattva* apparatus.

per_school.definition: *Pati* is Śiva, the *nimitta-kāraṇa* of the world; *paśu* is the bound soul, eternally distinct; *pāśa* is the bond. The end-state (*sāyujya*) is proximity to Śiva and the acquisition of the eight *aiśvaryas*, but not identity. The Pāśupata-Sūtra opens with this triad as the structural axis of the discipline.

*Pati*, *paśu*, *pāśa* are the three padārthas of the Saiddhāntika ontology, with *pāśa* analysed into the *kalā*-complex (the five contractive sheaths plus *māyā* plus *karma*). Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha's mature commentaries develop the triad against Pratyabhijñā non-dualism.

Somānanda, Utpaladeva, and Abhinavagupta argue that the three-fold separation of *pati*, *paśu*, *pāśa* cannot survive analysis of self-awareness and *svātantrya*: the *paśu*'s separation from *pati* is itself a moment of Śiva's self-limiting *māyā-śakti*, and the apparent triad is the differentiated self-display of one Śiva-consciousness. The Pratyabhijñā non-dualism is the Trika alternative to the Pāśupata-Saiddhāntika triad.

*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.2.37 refutes the *pāśupata-pakṣa*: Śiva-as-*nimitta-kāraṇa* without being *upādāna-kāraṇa* fails to give a coherent account of the Lord's relation to the world. Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, and Madhva all develop the argument with the Lakulīśa-Kauṇḍinya position in view.

translator_note: 'Lord-soul-bond' is the standard English rendering; the literal 'animal' for *paśu* is misleading in modern English (the Sanskrit term denotes a bound being, with the etymological 'tethered animal' connotation acceptable only with explanation). Western readers should not assimilate the Pāśupata position to monotheism without qualification — the Pāśupata Lord is *nimitta*-only, not the creator-substrate of the world.
